author: Origen of Alexandria
interpretation: Origen reads the 'spirits in prison' as the souls of the dead who heard Christ's preaching during his descent into Hades between crucifixion and resurrection. Christ went to the underworld to offer salvation to those who had died before his incarnation, including the generation of Noah. This fits Origen's broader theology of universal opportunity: no one is condemned without having had the chance to respond to the gospel, even if that opportunity comes after death.
context: Origen's reading contributed to the development of the doctrine of the harrowing of hell (descensus ad inferos), which entered the Apostles' Creed as 'he descended into hell.' His suggestion of post-mortem evangelism was more controversial and was eventually rejected by mainstream orthodoxy.

author: Augustine of Hippo
interpretation: Augustine rejected the idea of post-mortem evangelism. He argued that the pre-incarnate Christ (the divine Logos) preached through Noah to Noah's contemporaries while they were still alive. The 'spirits in prison' are those who are now in the prison of judgment, but they heard the preaching while alive on earth. The 'going' of Christ was spiritual, through the inspiration of Noah, not a physical descent to the underworld. This avoids any suggestion that there is a second chance after death.
context: Augustine's reading eliminated the troubling implications of post-mortem preaching and became the dominant Western interpretation until the Reformation. It influenced the Catholic Church's rejection of second-chance theology, though the Eastern tradition retained more openness to the harrowing-of-hell reading.

author: Thomas Aquinas
interpretation: Aquinas synthesized the patristic options: Christ's soul truly descended to the 'limbo of the fathers' (limbus patrum) after his death, where the righteous dead of the Old Testament awaited redemption. He preached to them not to convert them (they were already righteous) but to announce their liberation. The disobedient spirits of Noah's generation are in a different compartment of the underworld (hell proper), and Christ's descent proclaimed his triumph to them without offering them salvation.
context: Aquinas's careful compartmentalization of the underworld — limbus patrum, limbus infantium, purgatory, and hell — allowed him to affirm Christ's descent without implying universal post-mortem salvation. This became the standard Catholic position, formally affirmed at the Fourth Lateran Council.

author: John Calvin
interpretation: Calvin largely follows Augustine: the pre-incarnate Christ preached through Noah to the people of Noah's generation while they were alive. They are now 'spirits in prison' because they are confined in judgment. Calvin rejects both the Catholic doctrine of limbo and the idea of post-mortem evangelism. The point of the passage is encouragement: just as Noah was saved through water despite the mockery of his generation, so believers will be vindicated despite present persecution.
context: Calvin's reading stripped away the medieval cosmology of the underworld while retaining the Augustinian core. His emphasis on the passage's pastoral function — encouraging persecuted believers — shifted attention from speculative questions about the afterlife to the letter's immediate purpose.

author: Edward Gordon Selwyn
source_title: The First Epistle of St. Peter
interpretation: Selwyn proposed that the 'spirits in prison' are fallen angels from Genesis 6:1–4 (the 'sons of God' who took human wives), not the human dead. Christ's preaching to them was a proclamation of victory and judgment, not an offer of salvation. After his resurrection and ascension, Christ announced his triumph over the rebellious spiritual powers, demonstrating that the cosmic order has been decisively changed. The baptismal reference in verse 21 connects to the Flood as a type of salvation through judgment.
context: Selwyn's angelic interpretation drew on 1 Enoch's elaboration of the Genesis 6 tradition, which was well known in Second Temple Judaism and early Christianity. This reading has gained significant modern support because the Greek pneumata ('spirits') more naturally refers to supernatural beings than to human souls.

author: Karen Jobes
interpretation: Jobes favors the Enochic interpretation: Christ proclaimed his victory to the imprisoned fallen angels of the Genesis 6 and 1 Enoch tradition. She argues this fits the context of 1 Peter, which addresses Christians suffering unjustly: just as Christ triumphed over hostile spiritual powers, so believers can trust that their persecutors do not have the final word. The passage is fundamentally about Christ's cosmic authority, not about the fate of the human dead or post-mortem evangelism.
context: Jobes's commentary represents the growing consensus among evangelical scholars that the Enochic/angelic reading best accounts for the Greek vocabulary, the Second Temple Jewish background, and the letter's pastoral purpose. It avoids both the speculative excesses of the harrowing-of-hell tradition and the awkwardness of the Augustinian pre-incarnate preaching theory.

author: Scot McKnight
interpretation: McKnight acknowledges the passage is among the most difficult in the New Testament but leans toward the Enochic reading while noting the Augustinian option remains viable. He emphasizes the rhetorical function over the cosmological details: whatever exactly happened, the point is that Christ is victorious over all opposition, cosmic and human. For persecuted believers, the message is that no power — whether Roman authorities or spiritual forces — can ultimately overcome the one who has been raised and exalted to God's right hand.
context: McKnight's application-oriented approach reflects the practical concern that 1 Peter 3:18–20 should not become merely a puzzle for scholars but should function as it was intended: as encouragement for suffering believers. His reading illustrates the trend toward reading 1 Peter as a letter addressed to a real community in crisis.
